name: Angolar

ancestors.notes: Portuguese superstrate: thinner than other Gulf of Guinea creoles. Angolar descended from escaped enslaved Africans (primarily Kimbundu/Mbundu speakers) who fled into the interior of São Tomé in the 16th century; less contact with Portuguese colonizers.
São Tomé Portuguese adstrate via ongoing contact with the dominant variety of the island.

notes: Angolar (Nga). Portuguese-based Gulf of Guinea creole; spoken in the southern coast of São Tomé island by the Angolares, descendants of escaped enslaved Africans. ~5,000 speakers. Unique within the Gulf of Guinea creole cluster: much stronger Bantu substrate, lexical tone system, and thinner Portuguese superstrate. KEY FEATURES: (1) LEXICAL TONE: Angolar is tonal (H/L), unusual among Atlantic creoles and unique in the GGC cluster; tone not conventionally marked in writing. (2) LABIAL-VELAR STOPS: gb=[ɡ͡b], kp=[k͡p] — strong Niger-Congo substrate. (3) PRENASALIZED STOPS: mb/nd/ng. (4) NASAL VOWELS: marked with circumflex (â/ê/î/ô/û) in some orthographies. (5) SEVEN-VOWEL SYSTEM: a/e/ɛ/i/o/ɔ/u. (6) HEAVY BANTU VOCABULARY: more Kimbundu-derived vocabulary than any other PT creole. Sources: Maurer (1995) is the primary linguistic description.

sources.author: Maurer, P.
sources.title: L'Angolar: Un Créole Afro-Portugais parlé à São Tomé
sources.notes: Definitive grammar; includes full phonological description with tone

sources.author: Hagemeijer, T.
sources.title: The Gulf of Guinea creoles: genetic and typological relations
sources.publisher: Journal of Pidgin and Creole Languages 24(1)
